title_en: Al-Mudawwanah al-Kubra: The Foundational Text of Maliki Fiqh
title_ar: المدونة الكبرى: النص التأسيسي للفقه المالكي
content_en:
Al-Mudawwanah al-Kubra (The Great Codification) is the foundational text of Maliki jurisprudence, representing the first systematic recording of the legal opinions of Imam Malik ibn Anas (d. 179 AH / 795 CE) in a question-and-answer format. The work was compiled and organized by Abd al-Salam ibn Sa'id ibn Habib at-Tanukhi, known as Sahnun (d. 240 AH / 854 CE), a North African scholar who studied under Ibn al-Qasim al-Utaqi (d. 191 AH / 806 CE), one of Imam Malik's most important Egyptian students.

The history of Al-Mudawwanah's composition involves two stages. The first is the compilation of Al-Asadiyyah by Asad ibn al-Furat, a Maliki scholar from North Africa who traveled to Medina and Egypt to study under Malik's students and recorded their opinions in a question-and-answer format. Sahnun later revised and supplemented this compilation by consulting Ibn al-Qasim directly, adding Ibn al-Qasim's responses to new questions and correcting earlier records. The result, which Sahnun returned with to North Africa, became Al-Mudawwanah al-Kubra.

The format of Al-Mudawwanah — questions posed to Ibn al-Qasim, who transmits the answers he received from Malik or provides his own judgment when Malik's opinion was not available to him — preserves the living tradition of Maliki fiqh at its most formative stage. Unlike systematic legal treatises that organize material thematically, Al-Mudawwanah reflects the organic process by which legal opinions were developed and transmitted in the early period.

The authority of Al-Mudawwanah in Maliki jurisprudence is unparalleled. Later Maliki scholars described it as the 'mother book' (umm al-kutub) of the school, and their works are largely organized as engagements with its positions. The tradition of Maliki fiqh in North Africa and Al-Andalus, which became some of the most productive centers of Maliki scholarship, was built on the foundation of Al-Mudawwanah as transmitted and taught by Sahnun and his students.

Sahnun himself became one of the most important figures in North African Islamic scholarship. He served as a judge (qadi) in Kairouan and used his position to promote the Maliki school's establishment as the dominant legal tradition of the Maghrib. This institutional and scholarly combination was decisive in shaping the legal culture of North Africa for over a millennium.
